perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any information and documents (whether or no t designated as confidential), including, without limitation, finan cial, tax, commercial, corporate, legal, technical, operational, management, business and other informa tion and documents concerning the Target or any of their Affiliates, as well as any and all informatio n and documents regarding the intended sale of the Target, that is directly or indirectly disclosed, f urnished or made available, whether before or after the date hereof, in any way or by any means whatsoever (including, without limitation, in writing, orally or in electronic form), in a data room or elsewhere, to t he Receiving Party or any employees of the Receivin g Party, by any director, manager, agent, representat ive, advisor or employee of the Disclosing Party or any Affiliate of the Disclosing Party, in connection wi th the intended sale of the Target.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means any information and documents (whether or not designated as confidential), including, without limitation, financial, tax, commercial, corporate, legal, technical, operational, management, business and other information and documents concerning the Target or any of their Affiliates, as well as any and all information and documents regarding the intended sale of the Target, that is directly or indirectly disclosed, furnished or made available, whether before or after the date hereof, in any way or by any means whatsoever (including, without limitation, in writing, orally or in electronic form), in a data room or elsewhere, to the Receiving Party. For purposes of this agreement, the term Confidential Information specifically excludes trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of 'Confidential Information' within the contract, it creates a conflict if the information shared indeed qualifies as a trade secret under applicable laws. This misaligns the contract's definition with statutory definitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the UTSA, which defines trade secrets to include various forms of confidential business information. By explicitly excluding trade secrets, the contract potentially fails to protect information that would legally be considered a trade secret, thus undermining its protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This directly contradicts the underlying intent of trade secret law, which aims to protect such information. According to the provided snippet from 18 USC 1839, which defines terms used in the chapter regarding the protection of trade secrets, the exclusion undermines the protection of information that would legally be considered a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Parties agree and acknowledge that the informat ion defined hereunder as Confidential Information is a commercial secret of the Disclosin g Party, as it has commercial value in view of it being unknown and not publicly accessible.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Parties agree and acknowledge that the information defined hereunder as Confidential Information is *not* a commercial secret of the Disclosing Party, regardless of its commercial value or public accessibility.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating that the information is *not* a commercial secret, the contract directly contradicts the potential for the information to be protected as such under applicable laws, even if it meets the legal criteria for a commercial secret.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules, Article 31, § 3101
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts laws that provide protection for trade secrets/commercial secrets. In New York, CPLR 3101 outlines the scope of discovery, including protection for trade secrets. By disclaiming that the information is a commercial secret, the parties may unintentionally waive legal protections afforded to such information, such as protections during discovery in litigation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 3101 (2022) - Scope of Disclosure. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 31 - Disclosure 3101 - Scope of Disclosure. Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 3101 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 3101. Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the subst
perturbation.justification: The original contract acknowledges information as a commercial secret, while the changed contract explicitly states it is *not* a commercial secret. The Law Explanation indicates that this disclaimer may unintentionally waive legal protections afforded to commercial secrets, potentially impacting discovery in litigation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, from New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 3101, discusses the 'Scope of Disclosure', and subsection (b) states that 'privileged matter shall not be obtainable'. By stating the information is 'not' a commercial secret, the changed text directly undermines the ability to claim privilege and protection during legal proceedings, creating a legal contradiction because it explicitly nullifies the possibility of the information being treated as a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: 13. In case the Receiving Party breaches this Agreement , the Disclosing Party, the Target or any of their Affiliates are entitled to receive from the R eceiving Party compensation of financial and moral damages, suffered by the Disclosing Party, th e Target or any of their Affiliates respectively, as a result of such breach by the Rec eiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. In case the Receiving Party breaches this Agreement, the Disclosing Party, the Target or any of their Affiliates are entitled to receive from the Receiving Party compensation of financial damages *only*, suffered by the Disclosing Party, the Target or any of their Affiliates respectively, as a result of such breach by the Receiving Party. Moral damages are explicitly waived.
perturbation.explanation: By waiving 'moral damages,' the contract potentially conflicts with jurisdictions where such damages are legally recoverable for breach of contract, particularly where the breach involves sensitive or personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts California Civil Code § 3333, which allows for recovery of all damages proximately caused by a breach of obligation. While the enforceability of 'moral damages' can vary, explicitly waiving them may preclude recovery that a court might otherwise allow based on the specific circumstances of the breach, especially if the breach caused emotional distress or reputational harm.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 2. Damages for Wrongs [3333 - 3343.7] ( Article 2 enacted 1872. ) 3333. For the breach of an obligation not arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, whether it could have been anticipated or not. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly waives 'moral damages,' which could be recoverable under California Civil Code § 3333 for breach of obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CIV 3333, states that for breach of an obligation not arising from contract, the measure of damages is the amount which will compensate for all detriment proximately caused thereby. The change removes a potential avenue for damage recovery, creating a clear contradiction with the broad compensatory scope described in the law, especially if the breach caused emotional distress or reputational harm.

perturbation.original_text: 15. This Agreement and all legal relations arising from it shall be governed by the laws of [ specify the jurisdiction ].
perturbation.changed_text: 15. This Agreement and all legal relations arising from it shall be governed by the laws of [specify the jurisdiction], except that any disputes relating to intellectual property shall be governed by the laws of [another jurisdiction].
perturbation.explanation: By creating a carve-out for intellectual property disputes to be governed by a different jurisdiction, the contract creates potential conflicts of law if the interpretation of the NDA itself hinges on intellectual property concepts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: First Restatement of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification can contradict the First Restatement of Conflict of Laws § 187 (Law of the Forum), which dictates that the court will usually apply its own rules of conflict of laws. The Restatement provides that the parties' choice of law governs unless it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. By splitting the choice of law between jurisdictions, especially for intellectual property, it introduces uncertainty and potential conflict, as one jurisdiction's interpretation of the contract's intellectual property provisions may differ significantly from the other's.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a carve-out for intellectual property disputes, stipulating they be governed by the laws of a different jurisdiction. This division creates a conflict of laws, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, and undermines the original agreement specifying a single governing law. Although the provided scraped snippets do not directly address this specific situation, the Perturbation Explanation indicates this change introduces potential conflicts of law if the interpretation of the NDA hinges on intellectual property concepts, thereby creating a legally questionable scenario and the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition states the contract becomes unenforceable due to contractual language deviating from legally recognized definitions.
